title: Absence seizures suppress sensory-evoked cortical responses: a ZTE-fMRI study in awake GAERS rats
abstract_themes: Awake rat EEG-fMRI with silent ZTE sequence enables imaging of spontaneous absence seizures
Visual and whisker stimulation produce robust cortical, thalamic, and subcortical responses in the interictal state
During ictal spike-and-wave discharges, stimulation elicits paradoxical hemodynamic decreases in sensory cortices
Effect is regionally specific and significant across visual (p<0.001) and somatosensory (p<0.001) pathways

sections.name: Introduction
goals: Introduce absence epilepsy and spike-and-wave discharges (SWDs) as a model of brief loss of consciousness
Review the behavioral and electrophysiological evidence that sensory perception is impaired during SWDs
Highlight the gap: whole-brain hemodynamic mapping of sensory responsiveness during awake spontaneous SWDs has not been possible in rats due to MRI gradient noise
Motivate the ZTE-fMRI approach and its quiet/silent scanning advantages
State the core question: how do SWDs modulate sensory-evoked hemodynamic responses across the whole brain in awake GAERS rats?
Summarize contributions

sections.name: Related Work
goals: Animal models of absence epilepsy (GAERS, WAG/Rij)
Prior fMRI studies of SWDs (mostly under anesthesia or human EEG-fMRI)
Awake rodent fMRI: EPI vs SWIFT vs ZTE
Sensory processing during SWDs: EEG/ECoG evidence, behavioral tasks
Hemodynamic decreases during SWDs in cortex

sections.name: Methods
goals: GAERS rats (n=11, 8-12 months, 260 +/- 21 g, 6M/5F)
EEG electrode implantation (motor cortex AP:+2, ML:+2.5; primary somatosensory AP:-2.5, ML:+3; cerebellar ref AP:-12, ML:+2)
Habituation protocol (15 to 60 min over 8 days)
9.4T Bruker Biospec; T1-FLASH anatomical; 3D ZTE fMRI (TR 0.971 ms, flip 4 deg, matrix 60x60x60, 0.5x0.5x1 mm3, 1350 volumes, 45 min)
Visual stimulation: 3 Hz, 6 s, 166 ms pulses, 470 nm blue LED, n=14 sessions, 5 rats
Whisker stimulation: 2 Hz, 6 s, 250 ms pulses, 2.3 bar air, n=14 sessions, 4 rats
EEG at 1024 Hz; SWD detection 7-12 Hz, at least 2x baseline amplitude
Motion analysis; stimulation classification relative to SWDs
Statistical analysis (SPM cluster-level p<0.05, F-contrast, ROI beta-value extraction)

sections.name: Results
goals: Motion and quality metrics: translation 0.69 +/- 0.32 um; rotation 0.79 +/- 0.55 deg; 0.43 +/- 0.45 motions/min (vs 1.0 +/- 0.20 in prior EPI study); ZTE signal change ~0.3%
Session exclusions: 6/28 (21%) for motion, 4 (14%) no seizures, 1 (4%) technical; animal attrition 3/11 (27%) noisy EEG, 1% lost implant
Respiration stability: 2.2 +/- 0.4 Hz (visual), 2.0 +/- 0.3 Hz (whisker)
Interictal visual responses in V1, superior colliculus, LGN, frontal cortex (prelimbic, cingulate, secondary motor)
Interictal whisker responses in S1 barrel field, VPM thalamus, auditory cortex, medial frontal cortex
Interictal vs ictal: 136% more activated voxels in interictal state
Ictal vs interictal contrast: significant decreases in visual, somatosensory, medial frontal cortices
ROI beta-values, visual cortex: 2.8 +/- 1.7 baseline vs -6.0 +/- 2.0 ictal (p<0.001); stimulation-ending-seizure 8.1 +/- 7.0 vs -6.0 +/- 2.0 (p<0.001)
ROI beta-values, barrel cortex: 4.1 +/- 1.9 baseline vs -9.0 +/- 1.9 ictal (p<0.001); stimulation-ending-seizure 4.8 +/- 2.9 vs -9.0 +/- 1.9 (p<0.001)
Whole-brain ictal vs interictal stimulation contrast: significant changes in frontal, parietal, occipital regions (895 voxels)

sections.name: Discussion
goals: Summarize principal finding: SWDs actively suppress sensory-evoked hemodynamic responses, with ictal beta-values inverting sign
Interpret the paradoxical hemodynamic decrease as reflecting the 'wave' phase of SWDs dominating cortical responsiveness
Compare to Chipaux et al. 2013 and prior human EEG-fMRI work on SWDs
Discuss why stimulation-ending-seizure blocks show restored positive responses (8.1 +/- 7.0 and 4.8 +/- 2.9 beta)
Technical advantage of ZTE fMRI in awake rats: motion reduction vs EPI (0.43 vs 1.0 motions/min)
Limitations: smaller BOLD contrast of ZTE (~0.3% vs 1-1.5%), sample size, cluster-level thresholding
Implications for understanding transient loss of consciousness

queries: awake rodent fMRI zero echo time ZTE
